# Jairo Portfolio Azure Board Project

## Role

You are an Agile Project Manager consultant with 10+ years of experience in auditing projects using the SAFe framework [https://ScaledAgileFramework.com](https://ScaledAgileFramework.com).

## Goal

Your goal is to audit the project in Azure Board.

## Rules

```gherkin
-Scenario: Creating an audit report
  Given an audit report is created
  Then it should be saved under the `./audit/` sub-folder
  And it should follow the file naming convention of `AUDIT_<date>_<time>.md`

-Scenario: Adhering to framework standards
  Given the project is being audited or mentored
  Then the auditor must always follow SAFe framework standards and best practices

-Scenario: Visualizing data
  Given data is being presented in the report
Then the auditor must always create diagrams to visualize the data

-Scenario: Generating PDF reports
  Given an audit report is being generated as a PDF
  Then it must be saved in the same folder as the audit report
```

# SAFe Audit Report — Jairosoft Portfolio **Audit Date:** 2026-02-25 **Auditor:** Claude AI (Agile PM Consultant) **Framework:** Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) 6.0 **Organization:** Jairosoft LLC (`dev.azure.com/jairo`) **Project:** Jairosoft Portfolio --- ## 1. Executive Summary The Jairosoft Portfolio project is a multi-team, multi-product initiative managed through Azure DevOps with SAFe-aligned practices. The project spans 21 teams organized across Agile Release Trains (ARTs), with work structured through Program Increments (PIs). The portfolio currently operates across two primary product domains — **Colina Health** and **Sitecore** — alongside training, marketing, and operational functions delivered through the **Jairo Institute of Technology (JIT)**. ### Overall SAFe Maturity Assessment | Dimension | Score | Rating | |---|---|---| | Team Organization | 7/10 | Good | | PI Cadence & Iterations | 8/10 | Strong | | Backlog Hierarchy | 8/10 | Strong | | Work Item Discipline | 5/10 | Needs Improvement | | CI/CD Pipeline Health | 6/10 | Moderate | | Lean Portfolio Management | 7/10 | Good | **Overall Score: 6.8 / 10 — Moderate SAFe Maturity** --- ## 2. Organizational Structure The project has **21 teams**, which maps well to a SAFe Agile Release Train (ART) structure. ### Team Categories ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Portfolio Level" LPM["Jairosoft Lean Portfolio Management"] PM["Program Management Team"] end subgraph "Agile Teams (Development)" B["Bravo Agile Team"] W["Whiskey Agile Team"] U["Unicorn Agile Team"] C["Charlie Agile Team"] end subgraph "Product Teams" JIT["JIT Site eLMS Product Team"] CH["Colina Health Product Team"] MS["Marketing Site Product Team"] end subgraph "Shared Services" UX["UI UX Design Team"] DO["DevOps IT System Team"] AI["AI Coalition Team"] FO["FinOps SuccessForPeople Team"] MK["Marketing Sales Team"] TP["Technical Pre-Sales Team"] JO["JIT Operation Team"] JP["Jairosoft Project Team"] end subgraph "Intern Teams" IW["Interns Whiskey Team"] ID["Interns DevOps IT Team"] IU["Interns Unicorn Team"] IDe["Interns Design Team"] end LPM --> PM PM --> B & W & U & C PM --> JIT & CH & MS ``` ### Findings — Team Organization **Strengths:** - Clear SAFe hierarchy with a **Lean Portfolio Management** team at the top, which is a SAFe best practice for portfolio-level governance. - **Program Management Team** exists to coordinate across ARTs. - Named Agile teams (Bravo, Whiskey, Unicorn, Charlie) suggest structured team identity — a hallmark of healthy SAFe adoption. - Dedicated **Product Teams** for each value stream (JIT eLMS, Colina Health, Marketing Site). - **Shared Services** teams (DevOps IT, AI Coalition, UI/UX Design) properly support cross-cutting concerns. **Concerns:** - **21 teams is high** for a single ART. SAFe recommends 5–12 teams per ART. Consider whether this portfolio should be restructured into **2–3 ARTs** (e.g., Colina Health ART, JIT/Education ART, Marketing/Sales ART). - **4 Intern teams** may create coordination overhead. Consider embedding interns within established Agile teams rather than maintaining separate teams. - Several teams lack descriptions, making it hard to understand their scope and charter. --- ## 3. PI Cadence & Iteration Structure The portfolio follows a well-defined PI cadence with the following timeline: ### PI Timeline | PI | Start Date | End Date | Duration | Status | |---|---|---|---|---| | PI 0 | Sep 16, 2024 | Dec 29, 2024 | ~15 weeks | Completed | | PI 1 | Dec 30, 2024 | Mar 23, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 2 | Mar 24, 2025 | Jun 15, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 3 | Jun 16, 2025 | Sep 07, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 4 | Sep 08, 2025 | Nov 30, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 5 | Dec 01, 2025 | Feb 22, 2026 | ~12 weeks | **Just Completed** | ### PI 3 Iteration Breakdown (Detail Available) ```mermaid gantt title PI 3 Iteration Schedule dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD section PI 3 Iteration 3.1 :2025-06-16, 2025-06-29 Iteration 3.2 :2025-06-30, 2025-07-13 Iteration 3.3 :2025-07-14, 2025-07-27 Iteration 3.4 :2025-07-28, 2025-08-10 Iteration 3.5 :2025-08-11, 2025-08-24 Iteration 3.6 (IP) :2025-08-25, 2025-09-07 ``` ### Findings — PI Cadence **Strengths:** - Consistent **12-week PI cadence** from PI 1 onward (PI 0 was a 15-week onboarding/setup PI, which is acceptable). - PI 3 shows proper **2-week iteration cadence** with 5 development iterations + 1 Innovation & Planning (IP) iteration — this is textbook SAFe. - The IP iteration (3.6) is correctly labeled and positioned at the end of the PI. - **6 PIs completed** in ~17 months demonstrates commitment and velocity. **Concerns:** - As of Feb 25, 2026, PI 5 just ended (Feb 22). No PI 6 iteration structure is visible — this suggests **PI Planning may be delayed** or hasn't been configured yet in ADO. - PI 0 was 3 weeks longer than subsequent PIs. While acceptable for a launch PI, the variance should be documented. - Only PI 3 has visible sub-iterations; PIs 0, 1, 2, 4, and 5 do not show iteration breakdowns at the portfolio level. **Recommendation:** Ensure PI 6 planning is scheduled immediately. The 3-day gap between PIs should be used for Inspect & Adapt (I&A) and PI Planning events. --- ## 4. Backlog Hierarchy & Work Item Types The portfolio uses a customized SAFe backlog hierarchy: ```mermaid graph TD L5["Goals & Releases
(Goal, Release)"] L4["Epics & Objectives
(Epic, PI Objective)"] L3["Features & Enablers
(Feature, Enabler Feature, Change Request)"] L2["Stories & Deliverables
(User Story, Enabler, Spike, Defect,
Courseware, Training, Design, Tech Debt)"] L1["Tasks
(Task, Peer Review)"] L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1 style L5 fill:#60af49,color:#fff style L4 fill:#E06C00,color:#fff style L3 fill:#773B93,color:#fff style L2 fill:#0098C7,color:#fff style L1 fill:#A4880A,color:#fff ``` ### Findings — Backlog Hierarchy **Strengths:** - **5-level hierarchy** aligns well with SAFe's portfolio, large solution, program, and team levels. - Custom work item types like **Courseware**, **Training**, and **Design** show thoughtful adaptation of SAFe to the organization's domain (education/training). - **PI Objective** as a work item type at the Epic level is a strong SAFe practice. - **Risks and Impediments** backlog (Issue, Risk) is defined — this supports ROAM risk management. - **Peer Review** as a task type promotes quality practices. **Concerns:** - The **Features and Enablers** and **Stories and Deliverables** backlogs are marked as **hidden** (`isHidden: true`). This could mean teams aren't using the board views at these levels, losing visibility. - The **Risks and Impediments** backlog is also hidden — risk management visibility may be lacking. - **8 work item types** at the Story level is high. Consider whether Spike, Courseware, Training, and Design could be consolidated or tracked via tags instead. --- ## 5. Work Item Health Analysis Based on the 50 sampled work items: ### State Distribution | State | Count | % | |---|---|---| | Closed | 30 | 60% | | New | 8 | 16% | | Blocked | 3 | 6% | | Removed | 3 | 6% | | Requirements Gathering | 4 | 8% | | Grooming | 1 | 2% | | Other | 1 | 2% | ### Work Item Type Distribution ```mermaid pie title Work Item Types (Sampled) "Spike" : 21 "Enabler" : 8 "Training" : 4 "Courseware" : 5 "User Story" : 3 "Task" : 7 "Other" : 2 ``` ### Key Team Members | Team Member | Work Items | Primary Types | |---|---|---| | armelita@jairosoft.com | 13 | Training, Enabler, Courseware | | Jeffrey Avila | 7 | Spike (Marketing) | | Roche Casipong | 7 | Spike (Registration/Sales) | | grace@jairosoft.com | 5 | User Story, Courseware | | Elmer Joseph Cutin | 3 | Spike (Marketing) | | Vicsante Aseniero | 3 | Spike, Enabler | | Ramon Aseniero | 2 | Spike, User Story | ### Findings — Work Items **Strengths:** - **60% closure rate** on sampled items shows work is getting done. - Clear ownership — most work items have assigned team members. - Work spans multiple domains: marketing, training/courseware development, product development (Colina Health), and operations. **Critical Concerns:** - **Spikes dominate at 42%** of all work items. SAFe recommends spikes as time-boxed research activities, not the primary work type. This suggests the organization may be over-using Spikes instead of proper User Stories with acceptance criteria. - **User Stories are only 6%** of sampled work — this is alarmingly low. Value delivery should be primarily tracked through User Stories. - **3 Blocked items (6%)** with no visible resolution path. Blocked items tagged "OnHold" suggest systemic impediments that aren't being actively resolved. - **3 Removed items (6%)** indicate scope instability or poor backlog refinement. - **Items in "Requirements Gathering" and "Grooming" states** suggest some work items are languishing in pre-development stages. - **No Story Points visible** on any sampled work items — velocity tracking may not be active. --- ## 6. CI/CD Pipeline Health ### Pipeline Inventory (13 Pipelines) | Pipeline | Domain | Last Build | Status | |---|---|---|---| | BEColinaHealth.git Backend CICD | Colina Health | Feb 9, 2026 | Success | | colinahealth.git FrontEnd CICD | Colina Health | Feb 5, 2026 | Success | | Colina BE Docker Image | Colina Health | Mar 10, 2025 | Success | | Colina FE Docker Image | Colina Health | Mar 11, 2025 | Success | | ColinaHealth-API_Golang_Build | Colina Health | Mar 6, 2025 | Success | | colinahealth.git | Colina Health | Feb 5, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore Environment Creation | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore Environment Deletion | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | **Partial Failure** | | Sitecore-CD Repository Creation | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore-CM Repository Creation | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore Repository Deletion | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore | Sitecore | Jul 9, 2025 | Success | | SugConPH | Sitecore | Aug 15, 2025 | Success | ### Pipeline Activity Timeline ```mermaid gantt title Pipeline Last Build Activity dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD section Active (2026) BE Backend CICD :done, 2026-02-09, 1d FE Frontend CICD :done, 2026-02-05, 1d section Stale (2025) Colina BE Docker :crit, 2025-03-10, 1d Colina FE Docker :crit, 2025-03-11, 1d ColinaHealth-API Go :crit, 2025-03-06, 1d colinahealth.git :crit, 2025-02-05, 1d Sitecore Env Create :crit, 2025-08-14, 1d Sitecore Env Delete :crit, 2025-08-14, 1d Sitecore :crit, 2025-07-09, 1d SugConPH :crit, 2025-08-15, 1d ``` ### Findings — CI/CD **Strengths:** - **13 pipelines** across two product domains shows CI/CD investment. - The two most recent builds (Feb 2026) are for Colina Health, indicating active development on that product. - All builds except one show successful status. **Concerns:** - **11 of 13 pipelines haven't been triggered since 2025** — this means 85% of pipelines are stale. Either the codebases are dormant or builds aren't being triggered regularly. - **Sitecore pipelines** last ran in Aug 2025 (6+ months ago) — if Sitecore is still an active product, this is a red flag for continuous integration practices. - The **Sitecore Environment Deletion** pipeline shows a partial failure that was never addressed. - Only **Colina Health Backend and Frontend** pipelines show recent (2026) activity, suggesting development effort is concentrated on a single product. --- ## 7. Repository Analysis ### Repository Inventory (9 Repos) | Repository | Size | Domain | |---|---|---| | Sitecore-CD | 236 MB | Sitecore | | Sitecore-CM | 187 MB | Sitecore | | BEColinaHealth-GO | 42 MB | Colina Health | | SugConPH | 31 MB | Sitecore | | colinahealth.git | 30 MB | Colina Health | | FEColinaHealth-GO | 19 MB | Colina Health | | BEColinaHealth.git | 3.5 MB | Colina Health | | Sitecore | 2.6 MB | Sitecore | | JairoDevOps | <1 KB | DevOps | ### Findings — Repositories - **Sitecore repos are the largest** (423 MB combined) but show no recent pipeline activity — potential tech debt accumulation. - **Colina Health** has both legacy (.git suffix) and newer (GO suffix) repos, suggesting a technology migration from one stack to Go. - **JairoDevOps** repo is essentially empty (723 bytes) — should be cleaned up or documented. --- ## 8. SAFe Compliance Assessment ### SAFe Core Values Alignment | SAFe Core Value | Status | Evidence | |---|---|---| | **Alignment** | Partial | PI cadence exists; PI Objectives are defined as work item types. However, no visible Strategic Themes or portfolio Kanban flow. | | **Built-in Quality** | Needs Improvement | Peer Review task type exists, but no evidence of Definition of Done, automated testing metrics, or code review policies. | | **Transparency** | Partial | Work items are tracked, but key backlogs (Features, Stories, Risks) are hidden from board views. | | **Program Execution** | Good | 6 PIs completed with consistent cadence. Teams are structured and work is assigned. | ### SAFe Essential Practices Checklist | Practice | Implemented? | Notes | |---|---|---| | PI Planning | Likely | PI structure exists, but no formal PI Planning artifacts visible | | Iteration Planning | Partial | Only PI 3 shows sub-iterations | | Iteration Review/Demo | Unknown | No evidence in ADO | | Iteration Retrospective | Unknown | No evidence in ADO | | PI System Demo | Unknown | No evidence in ADO | | Inspect & Adapt | Unknown | No formal I&A artifacts visible | | Backlog Refinement | Partial | Items in "Grooming" state suggest some refinement | | Continuous Integration | Partial | Pipelines exist but most are stale | | Continuous Deployment | Partial | Docker-based pipelines suggest CD capability for Colina Health | | PI Objectives | Yes | PI Objective work item type defined at Epic level | | WSJF Prioritization | Unknown | No business value or time criticality data visible on items | --- ## 9. Recommendations ### Immediate Actions (This PI) 1. **Plan PI 6 immediately.** PI 5 ended Feb 22 — schedule PI Planning within the next week to avoid losing momentum. 2. **Address 3 Blocked work items.** Conduct an impediment removal session; items tagged "OnHold" need executive escalation or formal removal. 3. **Unhide Feature and Story backlogs.** Teams need board-level visibility at these levels for proper flow management. ### Short-Term (Next 1–2 PIs) 4. **Reduce Spike usage.** Convert ongoing research activities into proper User Stories with acceptance criteria. Target <15% Spikes in the backlog. 5. **Implement Story Points.** Without velocity data, predictability and capacity planning are impossible. 6. **Reactivate or archive stale pipelines.** 11 dormant pipelines create confusion; either trigger regular builds or formally decommission them. 7. **Restructure into 2–3 ARTs.** With 21 teams, consider splitting into Colina Health ART, JIT/Education ART, and Marketing/Operations ART. ### Long-Term (Next 2–4 PIs) 8. **Implement WSJF prioritization.** Add Business Value and Time Criticality fields to Features and Enablers. 9. **Embed interns into existing teams.** Reduce the 4 separate intern teams to improve mentoring and reduce coordination overhead. 10. **Establish Inspect & Adapt cadence.** Formalize I&A ceremonies at the end of each PI with documented improvement backlog. 11. **Clean up repository landscape.** Archive or document the empty JairoDevOps repo; assess whether legacy Colina Health repos (non-GO) should be deprecated. --- ## 10. Summary Scorecard ```mermaid quadrantChart title SAFe Maturity - Jairosoft Portfolio x-axis Low Maturity --> High Maturity y-axis Low Impact --> High Impact quadrant-1 Invest Now quadrant-2 Strong Foundation quadrant-3 Low Priority quadrant-4 Quick Wins "PI Cadence": [0.75, 0.85] "Team Structure": [0.65, 0.80] "Backlog Hierarchy": [0.70, 0.70] "Work Item Discipline": [0.35, 0.90] "CI/CD Health": [0.45, 0.75] "Velocity Tracking": [0.15, 0.85] "Risk Management": [0.30, 0.60] "LPM Governance": [0.60, 0.65] ``` --- *Report generated by Claude AI Agile PM Consultant on 2026-02-25. Based on SAFe 6.0 framework standards from [ScaledAgileFramework.com](https://ScaledAgileFramework.com).*

# SAFe Audit Report — Jairosoft Portfolio **Audit Date:** 2026-02-25 **Auditor:** Claude AI (Agile PM Consultant) **Framework:** Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) 6.0 **Organization:** Jairosoft LLC (`dev.azure.com/jairo`) **Project:** Jairosoft Portfolio --- ## 1. Executive Summary The Jairosoft Portfolio project is a multi-team, multi-product initiative managed through Azure DevOps with SAFe-aligned practices. The project spans 21 teams organized across Agile Release Trains (ARTs), with work structured through Program Increments (PIs). The portfolio currently operates across two primary product domains — **Colina Health** and **Sitecore** — alongside training, marketing, and operational functions delivered through the **Jairo Institute of Technology (JIT)**. ### Overall SAFe Maturity Assessment | Dimension | Score | Rating | |---|---|---| | Team Organization | 7/10 | Good | | PI Cadence & Iterations | 8/10 | Strong | | Backlog Hierarchy | 8/10 | Strong | | Work Item Discipline | 5/10 | Needs Improvement | | CI/CD Pipeline Health | 6/10 | Moderate | | Lean Portfolio Management | 7/10 | Good | **Overall Score: 6.8 / 10 — Moderate SAFe Maturity** --- ## 2. Organizational Structure The project has **21 teams**, which maps well to a SAFe Agile Release Train (ART) structure. ### Team Categories ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Portfolio Level" LPM["Jairosoft Lean Portfolio Management"] PM["Program Management Team"] end subgraph "Agile Teams (Development)" B["Bravo Agile Team"] W["Whiskey Agile Team"] U["Unicorn Agile Team"] C["Charlie Agile Team"] end subgraph "Product Teams" JIT["JIT Site eLMS Product Team"] CH["Colina Health Product Team"] MS["Marketing Site Product Team"] end subgraph "Shared Services" UX["UI UX Design Team"] DO["DevOps IT System Team"] AI["AI Coalition Team"] FO["FinOps SuccessForPeople Team"] MK["Marketing Sales Team"] TP["Technical Pre-Sales Team"] JO["JIT Operation Team"] JP["Jairosoft Project Team"] end subgraph "Intern Teams" IW["Interns Whiskey Team"] ID["Interns DevOps IT Team"] IU["Interns Unicorn Team"] IDe["Interns Design Team"] end LPM --> PM PM --> B & W & U & C PM --> JIT & CH & MS ``` ### Findings — Team Organization **Strengths:** - Clear SAFe hierarchy with a **Lean Portfolio Management** team at the top, which is a SAFe best practice for portfolio-level governance. - **Program Management Team** exists to coordinate across ARTs. - Named Agile teams (Bravo, Whiskey, Unicorn, Charlie) suggest structured team identity — a hallmark of healthy SAFe adoption. - Dedicated **Product Teams** for each value stream (JIT eLMS, Colina Health, Marketing Site). - **Shared Services** teams (DevOps IT, AI Coalition, UI/UX Design) properly support cross-cutting concerns. **Concerns:** - **21 teams is high** for a single ART. SAFe recommends 5–12 teams per ART. Consider whether this portfolio should be restructured into **2–3 ARTs** (e.g., Colina Health ART, JIT/Education ART, Marketing/Sales ART). - **4 Intern teams** may create coordination overhead. Consider embedding interns within established Agile teams rather than maintaining separate teams. - Several teams lack descriptions, making it hard to understand their scope and charter. --- ## 3. PI Cadence & Iteration Structure The portfolio follows a well-defined PI cadence with the following timeline: ### PI Timeline | PI | Start Date | End Date | Duration | Status | |---|---|---|---|---| | PI 0 | Sep 16, 2024 | Dec 29, 2024 | ~15 weeks | Completed | | PI 1 | Dec 30, 2024 | Mar 23, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 2 | Mar 24, 2025 | Jun 15, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 3 | Jun 16, 2025 | Sep 07, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 4 | Sep 08, 2025 | Nov 30, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 5 | Dec 01, 2025 | Feb 22, 2026 | ~12 weeks | **Just Completed** | ### PI 3 Iteration Breakdown (Detail Available) ```mermaid gantt title PI 3 Iteration Schedule dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD section PI 3 Iteration 3.1 :2025-06-16, 2025-06-29 Iteration 3.2 :2025-06-30, 2025-07-13 Iteration 3.3 :2025-07-14, 2025-07-27 Iteration 3.4 :2025-07-28, 2025-08-10 Iteration 3.5 :2025-08-11, 2025-08-24 Iteration 3.6 (IP) :2025-08-25, 2025-09-07 ``` ### Findings — PI Cadence **Strengths:** - Consistent **12-week PI cadence** from PI 1 onward (PI 0 was a 15-week onboarding/setup PI, which is acceptable). - PI 3 shows proper **2-week iteration cadence** with 5 development iterations + 1 Innovation & Planning (IP) iteration — this is textbook SAFe. - The IP iteration (3.6) is correctly labeled and positioned at the end of the PI. - **6 PIs completed** in ~17 months demonstrates commitment and velocity. **Concerns:** - As of Feb 25, 2026, PI 5 just ended (Feb 22). No PI 6 iteration structure is visible — this suggests **PI Planning may be delayed** or hasn't been configured yet in ADO. - PI 0 was 3 weeks longer than subsequent PIs. While acceptable for a launch PI, the variance should be documented. - Only PI 3 has visible sub-iterations; PIs 0, 1, 2, 4, and 5 do not show iteration breakdowns at the portfolio level. **Recommendation:** Ensure PI 6 planning is scheduled immediately. The 3-day gap between PIs should be used for Inspect & Adapt (I&A) and PI Planning events. --- ## 4. Backlog Hierarchy & Work Item Types The portfolio uses a customized SAFe backlog hierarchy: ```mermaid graph TD L5["Goals & Releases
(Goal, Release)"] L4["Epics & Objectives
(Epic, PI Objective)"] L3["Features & Enablers
(Feature, Enabler Feature, Change Request)"] L2["Stories & Deliverables
(User Story, Enabler, Spike, Defect,
Courseware, Training, Design, Tech Debt)"] L1["Tasks
(Task, Peer Review)"] L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1 style L5 fill:#60af49,color:#fff style L4 fill:#E06C00,color:#fff style L3 fill:#773B93,color:#fff style L2 fill:#0098C7,color:#fff style L1 fill:#A4880A,color:#fff ``` ### Findings — Backlog Hierarchy **Strengths:** - **5-level hierarchy** aligns well with SAFe's portfolio, large solution, program, and team levels. - Custom work item types like **Courseware**, **Training**, and **Design** show thoughtful adaptation of SAFe to the organization's domain (education/training). - **PI Objective** as a work item type at the Epic level is a strong SAFe practice. - **Risks and Impediments** backlog (Issue, Risk) is defined — this supports ROAM risk management. - **Peer Review** as a task type promotes quality practices. **Concerns:** - The **Features and Enablers** and **Stories and Deliverables** backlogs are marked as **hidden** (`isHidden: true`). This could mean teams aren't using the board views at these levels, losing visibility. - The **Risks and Impediments** backlog is also hidden — risk management visibility may be lacking. - **8 work item types** at the Story level is high. Consider whether Spike, Courseware, Training, and Design could be consolidated or tracked via tags instead. --- ## 5. Work Item Health Analysis Based on the 50 sampled work items: ### State Distribution | State | Count | % | |---|---|---| | Closed | 30 | 60% | | New | 8 | 16% | | Blocked | 3 | 6% | | Removed | 3 | 6% | | Requirements Gathering | 4 | 8% | | Grooming | 1 | 2% | | Other | 1 | 2% | ### Work Item Type Distribution ```mermaid pie title Work Item Types (Sampled) "Spike" : 21 "Enabler" : 8 "Training" : 4 "Courseware" : 5 "User Story" : 3 "Task" : 7 "Other" : 2 ``` ### Key Team Members | Team Member | Work Items | Primary Types | |---|---|---| | armelita@jairosoft.com | 13 | Training, Enabler, Courseware | | Jeffrey Avila | 7 | Spike (Marketing) | | Roche Casipong | 7 | Spike (Registration/Sales) | | grace@jairosoft.com | 5 | User Story, Courseware | | Elmer Joseph Cutin | 3 | Spike (Marketing) | | Vicsante Aseniero | 3 | Spike, Enabler | | Ramon Aseniero | 2 | Spike, User Story | ### Findings — Work Items **Strengths:** - **60% closure rate** on sampled items shows work is getting done. - Clear ownership — most work items have assigned team members. - Work spans multiple domains: marketing, training/courseware development, product development (Colina Health), and operations. **Critical Concerns:** - **Spikes dominate at 42%** of all work items. SAFe recommends spikes as time-boxed research activities, not the primary work type. This suggests the organization may be over-using Spikes instead of proper User Stories with acceptance criteria. - **User Stories are only 6%** of sampled work — this is alarmingly low. Value delivery should be primarily tracked through User Stories. - **3 Blocked items (6%)** with no visible resolution path. Blocked items tagged "OnHold" suggest systemic impediments that aren't being actively resolved. - **3 Removed items (6%)** indicate scope instability or poor backlog refinement. - **Items in "Requirements Gathering" and "Grooming" states** suggest some work items are languishing in pre-development stages. - **No Story Points visible** on any sampled work items — velocity tracking may not be active. --- ## 6. CI/CD Pipeline Health ### Pipeline Inventory (13 Pipelines) | Pipeline | Domain | Last Build | Status | |---|---|---|---| | BEColinaHealth.git Backend CICD | Colina Health | Feb 9, 2026 | Success | | colinahealth.git FrontEnd CICD | Colina Health | Feb 5, 2026 | Success | | Colina BE Docker Image | Colina Health | Mar 10, 2025 | Success | | Colina FE Docker Image | Colina Health | Mar 11, 2025 | Success | | ColinaHealth-API_Golang_Build | Colina Health | Mar 6, 2025 | Success | | colinahealth.git | Colina Health | Feb 5, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore Environment Creation | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore Environment Deletion | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | **Partial Failure** | | Sitecore-CD Repository Creation | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore-CM Repository Creation | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore Repository Deletion | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore | Sitecore | Jul 9, 2025 | Success | | SugConPH | Sitecore | Aug 15, 2025 | Success | ### Pipeline Activity Timeline ```mermaid gantt title Pipeline Last Build Activity dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD section Active (2026) BE Backend CICD :done, 2026-02-09, 1d FE Frontend CICD :done, 2026-02-05, 1d section Stale (2025) Colina BE Docker :crit, 2025-03-10, 1d Colina FE Docker :crit, 2025-03-11, 1d ColinaHealth-API Go :crit, 2025-03-06, 1d colinahealth.git :crit, 2025-02-05, 1d Sitecore Env Create :crit, 2025-08-14, 1d Sitecore Env Delete :crit, 2025-08-14, 1d Sitecore :crit, 2025-07-09, 1d SugConPH :crit, 2025-08-15, 1d ``` ### Findings — CI/CD **Strengths:** - **13 pipelines** across two product domains shows CI/CD investment. - The two most recent builds (Feb 2026) are for Colina Health, indicating active development on that product. - All builds except one show successful status. **Concerns:** - **11 of 13 pipelines haven't been triggered since 2025** — this means 85% of pipelines are stale. Either the codebases are dormant or builds aren't being triggered regularly. - **Sitecore pipelines** last ran in Aug 2025 (6+ months ago) — if Sitecore is still an active product, this is a red flag for continuous integration practices. - The **Sitecore Environment Deletion** pipeline shows a partial failure that was never addressed. - Only **Colina Health Backend and Frontend** pipelines show recent (2026) activity, suggesting development effort is concentrated on a single product. --- ## 7. Repository Analysis ### Repository Inventory (9 Repos) | Repository | Size | Domain | |---|---|---| | Sitecore-CD | 236 MB | Sitecore | | Sitecore-CM | 187 MB | Sitecore | | BEColinaHealth-GO | 42 MB | Colina Health | | SugConPH | 31 MB | Sitecore | | colinahealth.git | 30 MB | Colina Health | | FEColinaHealth-GO | 19 MB | Colina Health | | BEColinaHealth.git | 3.5 MB | Colina Health | | Sitecore | 2.6 MB | Sitecore | | JairoDevOps | <1 KB | DevOps | ### Findings — Repositories - **Sitecore repos are the largest** (423 MB combined) but show no recent pipeline activity — potential tech debt accumulation. - **Colina Health** has both legacy (.git suffix) and newer (GO suffix) repos, suggesting a technology migration from one stack to Go. - **JairoDevOps** repo is essentially empty (723 bytes) — should be cleaned up or documented. --- ## 8. SAFe Compliance Assessment ### SAFe Core Values Alignment | SAFe Core Value | Status | Evidence | |---|---|---| | **Alignment** | Partial | PI cadence exists; PI Objectives are defined as work item types. However, no visible Strategic Themes or portfolio Kanban flow. | | **Built-in Quality** | Needs Improvement | Peer Review task type exists, but no evidence of Definition of Done, automated testing metrics, or code review policies. | | **Transparency** | Partial | Work items are tracked, but key backlogs (Features, Stories, Risks) are hidden from board views. | | **Program Execution** | Good | 6 PIs completed with consistent cadence. Teams are structured and work is assigned. | ### SAFe Essential Practices Checklist | Practice | Implemented? | Notes | |---|---|---| | PI Planning | Likely | PI structure exists, but no formal PI Planning artifacts visible | | Iteration Planning | Partial | Only PI 3 shows sub-iterations | | Iteration Review/Demo | Unknown | No evidence in ADO | | Iteration Retrospective | Unknown | No evidence in ADO | | PI System Demo | Unknown | No evidence in ADO | | Inspect & Adapt | Unknown | No formal I&A artifacts visible | | Backlog Refinement | Partial | Items in "Grooming" state suggest some refinement | | Continuous Integration | Partial | Pipelines exist but most are stale | | Continuous Deployment | Partial | Docker-based pipelines suggest CD capability for Colina Health | | PI Objectives | Yes | PI Objective work item type defined at Epic level | | WSJF Prioritization | Unknown | No business value or time criticality data visible on items | --- ## 9. Recommendations ### Immediate Actions (This PI) 1. **Plan PI 6 immediately.** PI 5 ended Feb 22 — schedule PI Planning within the next week to avoid losing momentum. 2. **Address 3 Blocked work items.** Conduct an impediment removal session; items tagged "OnHold" need executive escalation or formal removal. 3. **Unhide Feature and Story backlogs.** Teams need board-level visibility at these levels for proper flow management. ### Short-Term (Next 1–2 PIs) 4. **Reduce Spike usage.** Convert ongoing research activities into proper User Stories with acceptance criteria. Target <15% Spikes in the backlog. 5. **Implement Story Points.** Without velocity data, predictability and capacity planning are impossible. 6. **Reactivate or archive stale pipelines.** 11 dormant pipelines create confusion; either trigger regular builds or formally decommission them. 7. **Restructure into 2–3 ARTs.** With 21 teams, consider splitting into Colina Health ART, JIT/Education ART, and Marketing/Operations ART. ### Long-Term (Next 2–4 PIs) 8. **Implement WSJF prioritization.** Add Business Value and Time Criticality fields to Features and Enablers. 9. **Embed interns into existing teams.** Reduce the 4 separate intern teams to improve mentoring and reduce coordination overhead. 10. **Establish Inspect & Adapt cadence.** Formalize I&A ceremonies at the end of each PI with documented improvement backlog. 11. **Clean up repository landscape.** Archive or document the empty JairoDevOps repo; assess whether legacy Colina Health repos (non-GO) should be deprecated. --- ## 10. Summary Scorecard ```mermaid quadrantChart title SAFe Maturity - Jairosoft Portfolio x-axis Low Maturity --> High Maturity y-axis Low Impact --> High Impact quadrant-1 Invest Now quadrant-2 Strong Foundation quadrant-3 Low Priority quadrant-4 Quick Wins "PI Cadence": [0.75, 0.85] "Team Structure": [0.65, 0.80] "Backlog Hierarchy": [0.70, 0.70] "Work Item Discipline": [0.35, 0.90] "CI/CD Health": [0.45, 0.75] "Velocity Tracking": [0.15, 0.85] "Risk Management": [0.30, 0.60] "LPM Governance": [0.60, 0.65] ``` --- *Report generated by Claude AI Agile PM Consultant on 2026-02-25. Based on SAFe 6.0 framework standards from [ScaledAgileFramework.com](https://ScaledAgileFramework.com).*

tool_input.new_string:
--- ## 11. Deep-Dive Audit: JIT Operation Team — Iteration 6.4 **Audit Focus:** JIT Operation Team, Iteration 6.4 (current sprint) **Sprint Window:** February 23, 2026 – March 8, 2026 (2 weeks) **Audit Date:** February 24, 2026 (Day 2 of Sprint) **PI Context:** 2026-PI6, Iteration 4 of 4 --- ### 11.1 Sprint Overview | Metric | Value | SAFe Benchmark | Status | |---|---|---|---| | Total Work Items | 17 | 8–12 per team per sprint | Over-committed | | Total Story Points | 23 pts (15 of 17 items estimated) | Based on team velocity | Unknown (no historical velocity) | | Team Members with Work | 3 (armelita, Samantha Babael, Teofilo Limpag) | 5–9 per agile team | Understaffed | | Configured Capacity | **0 hrs/day** | >0 required for burndown | Not configured | | Days Off Planned | 0 | -- | -- | | Risks/Impediments Logged | 0 | Track actively | Gap | ### 11.2 Capacity Analysis ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Team Capacity - Iteration 6.4" A["armelita
Capacity: 0 hrs/day
Items: 13
Points: 18"] S["Samantha Babael
Capacity: NOT CONFIGURED
Items: 3
Points: 1"] T["Teofilo Limpag
Capacity: NOT CONFIGURED
Items: 1
Points: 2"] end style A fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff style S fill:#e67e22,color:#fff style T fill:#f39c12,color:#fff ``` **Critical Finding:** Team capacity is configured at **0 hours/day for all members**. Only 2 members (armelita and grace) are registered as team members in Azure DevOps, yet grace has zero items in this sprint. Samantha Babael and Teofilo Limpag are contributing work but are **not registered as team members** — their capacity is invisible to the system. This means: - **Burndown charts are non-functional** (no capacity baseline) - **Sprint planning was done without capacity data** - **Predictability is impossible** — the team cannot measure if they are over- or under-committed --- ### 11.3 Sprint Backlog — Full Inventory #### Work Items by State ```mermaid pie title Iteration 6.4 — Work Item States "New" : 10 "Ready for Dev" : 2 "Ready" : 2 "Grooming" : 1 "Active" : 1 "Validation" : 1 ``` | # | ID | Type | Title | State | Assigned To | Points | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 199246 | User Story | Duplicate eLMS COC 1 | **Active** | Teofilo Limpag | 2 | | 2 | 197617 | User Story | Signing of Agreement on SK Buhangin Partnership | Ready for Dev | armelita | 1 | | 3 | 198612 | User Story | Follow up Sam Application as Trainer | Ready for Dev | armelita | 1 | | 4 | 198630 | Training | Markdown Training for the Employees | Ready | Samantha Babael | -- | | 5 | 199221 | Courseware | ChatGPT Courseware | Ready | Samantha Babael | -- | | 6 | 198637 | User Story | Markdown Training Dry-run | Grooming | Samantha Babael | 1 | | 7 | 198990 | Spike | Social Media Post for SOCOTECH Intern | Validation | Samantha Babael | 1 | | 8 | 198615 | User Story | Awarding of CSS NC II Certificates | New | armelita | 2 | | 9 | 199092 | User Story | Submit TESDA Career Guidance Semestral Report CY 2026 | New | armelita | 2 | | 10 | 199489 | User Story | Interview and Onboard Cor Jesu Interns | New | armelita | 2 | | 11 | 199496 | User Story | CSS NC II CTC SO Certificate | New | armelita | 1 | | 12 | 199498 | User Story | Get Copy of Lacking Admin Docs from Ma'am Grace | New | armelita | 1 | | 13 | 199499 | User Story | Update Company Profile for AC Compliance | New | armelita | 1 | | 14 | 199500 | User Story | Get Notarized Contract of Employees for AC Compliance | New | armelita | 1 | | 15 | 199501 | User Story | Get Copy of Building Layout, Shop Layout, and Floor Plan | New | armelita | 1 | | 16 | 199502 | User Story | Accomplish Checklist F04 AC Compliance | New | armelita | 1 | | 17 | 199503 | User Story | Repackage AC Compliance | New | armelita | 2 | > **Note:** Work item 199505 ("Contact Inquirers for their downpayment", 3 pts, armelita) and 198990 ("Social Media Post for SOCOTECH Intern", 1 pt, Samantha Babael) were also found in the Stories backlog assigned to Iteration 6.4 but 199505 did not appear in the iteration work items API query — this may indicate it was added after sprint start or has an iteration path mismatch. --- ### 11.4 Work Item Type Distribution | Type | Count | Story Points | % of Sprint | |---|---|---|---| | User Story | 13 | 19 | 76% | | Training | 1 | -- | 6% | | Courseware | 1 | -- | 6% | | Spike | 1 | 1 | 6% | | **Total** | **17** | **23** | **100%** | **Positive trend:** User Stories make up **76% of the sprint** — a significant improvement over the portfolio-wide 6% observed in the general audit (Section 5). This suggests the JIT Operation Team is better aligned with SAFe story-driven delivery practices. --- ### 11.5 Workload Distribution ```mermaid pie title Sprint Load by Team Member "armelita (13 items, 18 pts)" : 13 "Samantha Babael (3 items, 2 pts)" : 3 "Teofilo Limpag (1 item, 2 pts)" : 1 ``` | Team Member | Items | Story Points | % of Sprint Load | States | |---|---|---|---|---| | **armelita** | 13 | 18 pts | **76%** | 10 New, 2 Ready for Dev, 1 New | | Samantha Babael | 3 | 2 pts | 18% | 1 Ready, 1 Grooming, 1 Validation | | Teofilo Limpag | 1 | 2 pts | 6% | 1 Active | **Finding:** armelita is carrying **76% of the sprint** with **18 of 23 story points**. Of her 13 items, **10 are still in "New" state** on Day 2 of the sprint. This creates: 1. **Bus factor risk** — if armelita is unavailable, 76% of the sprint is blocked 2. **Flow bottleneck** — no work can progress through the board without her 3. **Potential over-commitment** — 18 points for a single person in 2 weeks, especially without historical velocity data --- ### 11.6 Sprint Theme Analysis The 17 work items cluster into 4 distinct themes: ```mermaid graph TD subgraph "Theme 1: TESDA AC Compliance (7 items, 9 pts)" A1["199499 - Update Company Profile"] A2["199500 - Notarized Contracts"] A3["199501 - Building Layouts"] A4["199502 - Checklist F04"] A5["199503 - Repackage AC Compliance"] A6["199496 - CSS NC II CTC SO Certificate"] A7["199498 - Get Admin Docs from Grace"] end subgraph "Theme 2: Training & Courseware (4 items, 1 pt)" B1["198630 - Markdown Training"] B2["198637 - Markdown Training Dry-run"] B3["199221 - ChatGPT Courseware"] B4["198990 - SOCOTECH Intern Social Media"] end subgraph "Theme 3: Partnerships & Onboarding (3 items, 5 pts)" C1["197617 - SK Buhangin Agreement"] C2["199489 - Cor Jesu Interns"] C3["198612 - Sam Trainer Application"] end subgraph "Theme 4: Certification & Reporting (3 items, 6 pts)" D1["198615 - CSS NC II Certificates"] D2["199092 - TESDA Semestral Report"] D3["199246 - Duplicate eLMS COC 1"] end style A1 fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff style A2 fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff style A3 fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff style A4 fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff style A5 fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff style A6 fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff style A7 fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff style B1 fill:#3498db,color:#fff style B2 fill:#3498db,color:#fff style B3 fill:#3498db,color:#fff style B4 fill:#3498db,color:#fff style C1 fill:#2ecc71,color:#fff style C2 fill:#2ecc71,color:#fff style C3 fill:#2ecc71,color:#fff style D1 fill:#9b59b6,color:#fff style D2 fill:#9b59b6,color:#fff style D3 fill:#9b59b6,color:#fff ``` **Observation:** The sprint is dominated by **TESDA Assessment Center (AC) compliance work** — 7 of 17 items (41%) are administrative/regulatory tasks for AC compliance. These are all assigned to armelita and all in "New" state. This represents a **regulatory deadline risk** if this compliance work has a hard due date. --- ### 11.7 State Flow Assessment ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 direction LR [*] --> New : 10 items New --> Grooming : 0 in transit Grooming --> Ready : 0 in transit Ready --> ReadyForDev : 0 in transit ReadyForDev --> Active : 0 in transit Active --> Validation : 0 in transit Validation --> Closed : 0 in transit state "New (10)" as New state "Grooming (1)" as Grooming state "Ready (2)" as Ready state "Ready for Dev (2)" as ReadyForDev state "Active (1)" as Active state "Validation (1)" as Validation ``` **Day 2 Sprint Health:** - **59% of items (10/17) are still in "New"** — they have not entered the team's workflow - **Only 1 item (6%) is "Active"** — actual work in progress - **1 item is in "Validation"** — nearing completion - **1 item is in "Grooming"** — should not be in the sprint if not yet groomed (SAFe: items entering a sprint should be "Ready") **SAFe Violation:** Item 198637 ("Markdown Training Dry-run") is in **Grooming** state inside an active sprint. Per SAFe, work entering an iteration should meet the team's Definition of Ready. Grooming should happen during backlog refinement *before* sprint planning, not during the sprint. --- ### 11.8 Estimation Quality | Metric | Value | Assessment | |---|---|---| | Items with Story Points | 15 of 17 (88%) | Good | | Items without Story Points | 2 (Training + Courseware) | Gap — all sprint items should be estimated | | Point range | 1–3 pts | Healthy — small, right-sized stories | | Average points per item | 1.5 pts | Low complexity work | | Total sprint commitment | 23 pts | Unknown if achievable (no velocity baseline) | **Positive:** Story point adoption is much higher in this sprint (88%) compared to the broader portfolio. Most items are sized at 1–2 points, indicating they are properly decomposed. **Gap:** The 2 unestimated items (Training and Courseware types) suggest the team does not estimate non-User Story types, which creates blind spots in velocity tracking. --- ### 11.9 SAFe Iteration Compliance Scorecard | SAFe Practice | Status | Evidence | |---|---|---| | **Iteration Planning** | Partial | Items are assigned to the iteration, but capacity is 0 and 59% of items are "New" — suggests items were added without a planning ceremony | | **Definition of Ready** | Not Enforced | 1 item in "Grooming" state is in the sprint; 10 items in "New" have not been pulled | | **Capacity-Based Planning** | Not Implemented | All team members show 0 hrs/day capacity | | **WIP Limits** | Not Visible | No evidence of WIP limits on the board | | **Daily Stand-up** | Unknown | No ADO evidence (expected — stand-ups are ceremonies, not tracked in ADO) | | **Iteration Review/Demo** | Unknown | Sprint ends Mar 8 — no demo scheduled visible in ADO | | **Story Point Estimation** | Mostly Implemented | 88% of items have estimates — good but should be 100% | | **Acceptance Criteria** | Unknown | Not visible from backlog query — would need item-level detail | | **Burndown Tracking** | Non-Functional | Capacity = 0 means burndown chart has no baseline | | **ROAM Risk Management** | Not Implemented | 0 risks/impediments logged for this team | --- ### 11.10 Iteration 6.4 — Risk Register | # | Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation | |---|---|---|---|---| | R1 | **Single point of failure (armelita)** — 76% of sprint depends on one person | High | Critical | Redistribute work; cross-train team members; add capacity | | R2 | **No capacity configured** — sprint commitment cannot be validated against available hours | Confirmed | High | Configure capacity for all active team members immediately | | R3 | **TESDA AC Compliance deadline** — 7 compliance items all in "New" on Day 2 | Medium | High | Clarify regulatory deadline; prioritize if time-bound | | R4 | **Grooming in-sprint** — item 198637 is not ready for development | Confirmed | Low | Complete grooming or remove from sprint | | R5 | **No velocity baseline** — team cannot assess if 23 pts is achievable | Confirmed | Medium | Track this sprint's actual completion to establish baseline | | R6 | **Unregistered team members** — Samantha Babael and Teofilo Limpag not in team roster | Confirmed | Medium | Add them to the JIT Operation Team in ADO settings | --- ### 11.11 Recommendations — Iteration 6.4 Specific #### Immediate (This Week) 1. **Configure team capacity.** Add armelita, Samantha Babael, and Teofilo Limpag with actual hours/day so the burndown chart becomes functional. 2. **Add Samantha Babael and Teofilo Limpag to the team roster** in Azure DevOps so their work is properly tracked. 3. **Resolve item 198637 (Markdown Training Dry-run).** Either complete grooming and move to "Ready" or remove from the sprint and place it in the next iteration. 4. **Estimate the 2 unestimated items** (198630 - Markdown Training, 199221 - ChatGPT Courseware) to get a complete sprint commitment. 5. **Clarify TESDA AC compliance deadline.** If there is a hard regulatory date, the 7 compliance items should be prioritized above other work. #### Before Sprint End (Mar 8) 6. **Track completion rate.** At sprint close, record how many of the 23 story points were completed — this becomes the team's first velocity data point. 7. **Conduct Iteration Review.** Demo completed work to stakeholders, especially the TESDA compliance deliverables. 8. **Conduct Iteration Retrospective.** Key topics should include: - Why was capacity not configured? - Is the workload distribution sustainable? - Are items entering sprints # SAFe Audit Report — Jairosoft Portfolio **Audit Date:** 2026-02-25 **Auditor:** Claude AI (Agile PM Consultant) **Framework:** Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) 6.0 **Organization:** Jairosoft LLC (`dev.azure.com/jairo`) **Project:** Jairosoft Portfolio --- ## 1. Executive Summary The Jairosoft Portfolio project is a multi-team, multi-product initiative managed through Azure DevOps with SAFe-aligned practices. The project spans 21 teams organized across Agile Release Trains (ARTs), with work structured through Program Increments (PIs). The portfolio currently operates across two primary product domains — **Colina Health** and **Sitecore** — alongside training, marketing, and operational functions delivered through the **Jairo Institute of Technology (JIT)**. ### Overall SAFe Maturity Assessment | Dimension | Score | Rating | |---|---|---| | Team Organization | 7/10 | Good | | PI Cadence & Iterations | 8/10 | Strong | | Backlog Hierarchy | 8/10 | Strong | | Work Item Discipline | 5/10 | Needs Improvement | | CI/CD Pipeline Health | 6/10 | Moderate | | Lean Portfolio Management | 7/10 | Good | **Overall Score: 6.8 / 10 — Moderate SAFe Maturity** --- ## 2. Organizational Structure The project has **21 teams**, which maps well to a SAFe Agile Release Train (ART) structure. ### Team Categories ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Portfolio Level" LPM["Jairosoft Lean Portfolio Management"] PM["Program Management Team"] end subgraph "Agile Teams (Development)" B["Bravo Agile Team"] W["Whiskey Agile Team"] U["Unicorn Agile Team"] C["Charlie Agile Team"] end subgraph "Product Teams" JIT["JIT Site eLMS Product Team"] CH["Colina Health Product Team"] MS["Marketing Site Product Team"] end subgraph "Shared Services" UX["UI UX Design Team"] DO["DevOps IT System Team"] AI["AI Coalition Team"] FO["FinOps SuccessForPeople Team"] MK["Marketing Sales Team"] TP["Technical Pre-Sales Team"] JO["JIT Operation Team"] JP["Jairosoft Project Team"] end subgraph "Intern Teams" IW["Interns Whiskey Team"] ID["Interns DevOps IT Team"] IU["Interns Unicorn Team"] IDe["Interns Design Team"] end LPM --> PM PM --> B & W & U & C PM --> JIT & CH & MS ``` ### Findings — Team Organization **Strengths:** - Clear SAFe hierarchy with a **Lean Portfolio Management** team at the top, which is a SAFe best practice for portfolio-level governance. - **Program Management Team** exists to coordinate across ARTs. - Named Agile teams (Bravo, Whiskey, Unicorn, Charlie) suggest structured team identity — a hallmark of healthy SAFe adoption. - Dedicated **Product Teams** for each value stream (JIT eLMS, Colina Health, Marketing Site). - **Shared Services** teams (DevOps IT, AI Coalition, UI/UX Design) properly support cross-cutting concerns. **Concerns:** - **21 teams is high** for a single ART. SAFe recommends 5–12 teams per ART. Consider whether this portfolio should be restructured into **2–3 ARTs** (e.g., Colina Health ART, JIT/Education ART, Marketing/Sales ART). - **4 Intern teams** may create coordination overhead. Consider embedding interns within established Agile teams rather than maintaining separate teams. - Several teams lack descriptions, making it hard to understand their scope and charter. --- ## 3. PI Cadence & Iteration Structure The portfolio follows a well-defined PI cadence with the following timeline: ### PI Timeline | PI | Start Date | End Date | Duration | Status | |---|---|---|---|---| | PI 0 | Sep 16, 2024 | Dec 29, 2024 | ~15 weeks | Completed | | PI 1 | Dec 30, 2024 | Mar 23, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 2 | Mar 24, 2025 | Jun 15, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 3 | Jun 16, 2025 | Sep 07, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 4 | Sep 08, 2025 | Nov 30, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 5 | Dec 01, 2025 | Feb 22, 2026 | ~12 weeks | **Just Completed** | ### PI 3 Iteration Breakdown (Detail Available) ```mermaid gantt title PI 3 Iteration Schedule dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD section PI 3 Iteration 3.1 :2025-06-16, 2025-06-29 Iteration 3.2 :2025-06-30, 2025-07-13 Iteration 3.3 :2025-07-14, 2025-07-27 Iteration 3.4 :2025-07-28, 2025-08-10 Iteration 3.5 :2025-08-11, 2025-08-24 Iteration 3.6 (IP) :2025-08-25, 2025-09-07 ``` ### Findings — PI Cadence **Strengths:** - Consistent **12-week PI cadence** from PI 1 onward (PI 0 was a 15-week onboarding/setup PI, which is acceptable). - PI 3 shows proper **2-week iteration cadence** with 5 development iterations + 1 Innovation & Planning (IP) iteration — this is textbook SAFe. - The IP iteration (3.6) is correctly labeled and positioned at the end of the PI. - **6 PIs completed** in ~17 months demonstrates commitment and velocity. **Concerns:** - As of Feb 25, 2026, PI 5 just ended (Feb 22). No PI 6 iteration structure is visible — this suggests **PI Planning may be delayed** or hasn't been configured yet in ADO. - PI 0 was 3 weeks longer than subsequent PIs. While acceptable for a launch PI, the variance should be documented. - Only PI 3 has visible sub-iterations; PIs 0, 1, 2, 4, and 5 do not show iteration breakdowns at the portfolio level. **Recommendation:** Ensure PI 6 planning is scheduled immediately. The 3-day gap between PIs should be used for Inspect & Adapt (I&A) and PI Planning events. --- ## 4. Backlog Hierarchy & Work Item Types The portfolio uses a customized SAFe backlog hierarchy: ```mermaid graph TD L5["Goals & Releases
(Goal, Release)"] L4["Epics & Objectives
(Epic, PI Objective)"] L3["Features & Enablers
(Feature, Enabler Feature, Change Request)"] L2["Stories & Deliverables
(User Story, Enabler, Spike, Defect,
Courseware, Training, Design, Tech Debt)"] L1["Tasks
(Task, Peer Review)"] L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1 style L5 fill:#60af49,color:#fff style L4 fill:#E06C00,color:#fff style L3 fill:#773B93,color:#fff style L2 fill:#0098C7,color:#fff style L1 fill:#A4880A,color:#fff ``` ### Findings — Backlog Hierarchy **Strengths:** - **5-level hierarchy** aligns well with SAFe's portfolio, large solution, program, and team levels. - Custom work item types like **Courseware**, **Training**, and **Design** show thoughtful adaptation of SAFe to the organization's domain (education/training). - **PI Objective** as a work item type at the Epic level is a strong SAFe practice. - **Risks and Impediments** backlog (Issue, Risk) is defined — this supports ROAM risk management. - **Peer Review** as a task type promotes quality practices. **Concerns:** - The **Features and Enablers** and **Stories and Deliverables** backlogs are marked as **hidden** (`isHidden: true`). This could mean teams aren't using the board views at these levels, losing visibility. - The **Risks and Impediments** backlog is also hidden — risk management visibility may be lacking. - **8 work item types** at the Story level is high. Consider whether Spike, Courseware, Training, and Design could be consolidated or tracked via tags instead. --- ## 5. Work Item Health Analysis Based on the 50 sampled work items: ### State Distribution | State | Count | % | |---|---|---| | Closed | 30 | 60% | | New | 8 | 16% | | Blocked | 3 | 6% | | Removed | 3 | 6% | | Requirements Gathering | 4 | 8% | | Grooming | 1 | 2% | | Other | 1 | 2% | ### Work Item Type Distribution ```mermaid pie title Work Item Types (Sampled) "Spike" : 21 "Enabler" : 8 "Training" : 4 "Courseware" : 5 "User Story" : 3 "Task" : 7 "Other" : 2 ``` ### Key Team Members | Team Member | Work Items | Primary Types | |---|---|---| | armelita@jairosoft.com | 13 | Training, Enabler, Courseware | | Jeffrey Avila | 7 | Spike (Marketing) | | Roche Casipong | 7 | Spike (Registration/Sales) | | grace@jairosoft.com | 5 | User Story, Courseware | | Elmer Joseph Cutin | 3 | Spike (Marketing) | | Vicsante Aseniero | 3 | Spike, Enabler | | Ramon Aseniero | 2 | Spike, User Story | ### Findings — Work Items **Strengths:** - **60% closure rate** on sampled items shows work is getting done. - Clear ownership — most work items have assigned team members. - Work spans multiple domains: marketing, training/courseware development, product development (Colina Health), and operations. **Critical Concerns:** - **Spikes dominate at 42%** of all work items. SAFe recommends spikes as time-boxed research activities, not the primary work type. This suggests the organization may be over-using Spikes instead of proper User Stories with acceptance criteria. - **User Stories are only 6%** of sampled work — this is alarmingly low. Value delivery should be primarily tracked through User Stories. - **3 Blocked items (6%)** with no visible resolution path. Blocked items tagged "OnHold" suggest systemic impediments that aren't being actively resolved. - **3 Removed items (6%)** indicate scope instability or poor backlog refinement. - **Items in "Requirements Gathering" and "Grooming" states** suggest some work items are languishing in pre-development stages. - **No Story Points visible** on any sampled work items — velocity tracking may not be active. --- ## 6. **SAFe Guidance:** SAFe recommends work be distributed across team members to promote collective ownership and reduce dependency risk. No single team member should own more than 30–40% of a sprint's committed work.

---

## 14. Conclusion

The JIT Operation Team demonstrates **promising SAFe fundamentals** — properly typed PI Objectives, story-driven delivery (82% User Stories), reasonable story point adoption (88%), and right-sized work items (1–3 points). These are signs of a team that understands the mechanics of agile delivery.

However, **critical process gaps** prevent the team from realizing the benefits of these fundamentals:

1. **Zero configured capacity** makes planning and tracking meaningless
2. **76% workload concentration on one person** creates unacceptable organizational risk
3. **No risk management** leaves the team blind to impediments
4. **Stale backlog items** (8+ months) indicate insufficient refinement discipline
5. **No IP iteration** removes protected time for improvement

The path forward is clear: **fix capacity and workload balance first** (Quick Wins), then **establish velocity and refinement cadence** (Short Term), and finally **implement WSJF and formalize I&A** (Medium Term). The team has the foundation — it now needs the discipline.

---

| 12 | 199499 | User Story | Update Company Profile for AC Compliance | New | armelita | 1 |
| 13 | 199500 | User Story | Get Notarized Contract of Employees for AC Compliance | New | armelita | 1 |
| 14 | 199501 | User Story | Get Copy of Building Layout, Shop Layout, and Floor Plan | New | armelita | 1 |
| 15 | 199502 | User Story | Accomplish Checklist F04 AC Compliance | New | armelita | 1 |
| 16 | 199503 | User Story | Repackage AC Compliance | New | armelita | 2 |
| 17 | 199505 | User Story | Contact Inquirers for their downpayment | New | armelita | 3 |

### 5.3 Work Item State Distribution

```mermaid
pie title Iteration 6.4 — Work Item States (Day 2)
    "New" : 10
    "Ready for Dev" : 2
    "Ready" : 2
    "Grooming" : 1
    "Active" : 1
```

**Analysis:**
- **59% New** — The majority of sprint work has not entered the team's workflow. On Day 2 of a 14-day sprint, this signals either late sprint planning or work items that were added without proper commitment.
- **12% Ready for Dev** — 2 items are refined and ready to be picked up.
- **12% Ready** — 2 items (Training + Courseware) are prepped but haven't been pulled.
- **6% Grooming** — 1 item is still being refined inside the sprint. **This is a SAFe violation** — items should meet Definition of Ready before entering an iteration.
- **6% Active** — Only 1 item has actual work in progress.

| Courseware | 1 | -- | 6% |
| Spike | 1 | -- | 6% |
| **Total** | **17** | **23** | **100%** |

**Positive Finding:** User Stories account for **82% of the sprint**. This is a major improvement over the portfolio-wide observation where Spikes dominated at 42% and User Stories were only 6%. The JIT Operation Team is properly using User Stories as the primary vehicle for value delivery — a SAFe best practice.

---

## 6. Workload Distribution Analysis

| Samantha Babael | 3 | 2 pts | 18% | 1 Ready, 1 Grooming, 1 Ready |
| Teofilo Limpag | 1 | 2 pts | 6% | 1 Active |
| **Total** | **17** | **23 pts** | **100%** | |

### 6.2 Workload Imbalance Visualization

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Story Points by Team Member"
    x-axis ["armelita", "Samantha Babael", "Teofilo Limpag"]
    y-axis "Story Points" 0 --> 20
    bar [18, 2, 2]
```

### 6.3 Finding — Critical Workload Imbalance

**armelita carries 76% of all sprint work (13 items, 18 of 23 story points).** This is the single most critical finding in this audit.

| Risk Factor | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Bus Factor** | If armelita is unavailable for even 2–3 days, 76% of the sprint is at risk |
| **Bottleneck** | All 10 "New" items are hers — nothing moves until she starts them |
| **Burnout Risk** | 18 story points in 10 working days = ~1.8 pts/day sustained |
| **Quality Risk** | High volume + single person = reduced time for thorough work |

**SAFe Guidance:** SAFe recommends work be distributed across team members to promote collective ownership and reduce dependency risk. No single team member should own more than 30–40% of a sprint's committed work.

---

## 7. Sprint Theme Analysis

The 17 work items cluster into **4 strategic themes**:

```mermaid
graph TD
```

| Theme | Items | Points | Owner(s) | Risk Level |

| Training & Courseware | 3 (18%) | 1 | Samantha Babael | Low |
| Partnerships & Onboarding | 4 (24%) | 7 | armelita (100%) | Medium |
| Certification & Reporting | 3 (18%) | 6 | Mixed | Medium |

**Key Insight:** The sprint is dominated by **TESDA Assessment Center compliance** (41% of items). These 7 items form a dependency cluster — they all relate to preparing documentation for regulatory compliance. If there is a hard TESDA deadline, these should be prioritized above all other work. Currently, all 7 are in "New" state on Day 2.

## 8. Capacity Analysis

### 8.1 Configured Capacity

| Member | Capacity/Day | Days Off | Role |

| Teofilo Limpag | **Not in roster** | -- | Contributing but unregistered |

### 8.2 Cross-Team Comparison (Iteration 6.4)

| Team | Capacity/Day | Days Off |
|---|---|---|
| Colina Health Product Team | 14.5 hrs | 2 |
| JIT Site eLMS Product Team | 48 hrs | 0 |
| **JIT Operation Team** | **0 hrs** | **0** |

**Finding:** The JIT Operation Team is the **only team with zero configured capacity** among the teams active in Iteration 6.4. Other teams (Colina Health at 14.5 hrs/day, JIT eLMS at 48 hrs/day) have properly configured capacity, demonstrating that the organization knows how to set this up — the JIT Operation Team simply hasn't done it.

### 8.3 Impact of Zero Capacity

Without configured capacity:
1. **Burndown charts** show no baseline — the team cannot track progress visually
2. **Sprint commitment validation** is impossible — there is no way to know if 23 points is achievable
3. **Azure DevOps capacity alerts** will not trigger — the system cannot warn about over-allocation
4. **Velocity tracking** has no anchor — historical capacity-to-delivery ratios cannot be calculated

---

## 9. Backlog Health — Full Board Analysis

**Finding:** The board schema includes **Effort** and **Business Value** fields at the Feature level (required for WSJF calculation), but **none of the 36 Features have values populated**. This means **Weighted Shortest Job First (WSJF) prioritization is impossible** — a core SAFe Lean Portfolio Management practice.

## 10. State Flow & Process Compliance

### 10.1 Workflow States Observed

The team uses the following workflow states:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    state "New" as New
    state "Requirements Gathering" as RequirementsGathering
    state "Grooming" as Grooming
    state "Ready" as Ready
    state "Ready for Dev" as ReadyForDev
    state "Active" as Active
    state "Validation" as Validation
    state "Closed" as Closed
```

### 10.2 SAFe Process Violations

| Violation | Item(s) | SAFe Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Item in "Grooming" inside active sprint | 198637 (Markdown Training Dry-run) | Items must meet Definition of Ready before entering an iteration |
| PI Objectives still "New" in final iteration | 197444, 197446, 197450 | PI Objectives should be actively tracked throughout the PI |
| Zero risks logged | Entire board | Teams should track risks using ROAM (Resolved, Owned, Accepted, Mitigated) |
| No capacity configured | All team members | Capacity must be set for burndown and commitment validation |
| Stale items from prior PIs | 10 items from PI 3 | Backlog refinement should clean up stale items each PI |

---

## 11. Risk Register

| # | Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Category | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **R1** | **Single point of failure** — armelita owns 76% of sprint work | High | Critical | People | Redistribute work; cross-train Samantha and Teofilo; add team members |
| **R2** | **Zero capacity configured** — sprint commitment is unvalidated | Confirmed | High | Process | Configure capacity for armelita, Samantha, and Teofilo immediately |
| **R3** | **TESDA AC Compliance deadline risk** — 7 regulatory items all "New" on Day 2 | Medium | High | Regulatory | Clarify TESDA deadline; if time-bound, prioritize above all other sprint work |
| **R4** | **PI Objectives at risk** — 50% still "New" in final iteration | High | Medium | Delivery | Assess PI Objective achievability; document unmet objectives for I&A |
| **R5** | **Unregistered team members** — Samantha and Teofilo not in ADO roster | Confirmed | Medium | Governance | Add them to the JIT Operation Team in ADO project settings |
| **R6** | **Backlog debt** — 20 stale items (PI 3 + root) creating noise | Confirmed | Low | Process | Conduct backlog refinement session; remove or re-plan stale items |
| **R7** | **No velocity baseline** — team cannot forecast future sprints | Confirmed | Medium | Planning | Track Iteration 6.4 actuals to establish first velocity data point |
| **R8** | **No IP iteration in PI 6** — no protected time for innovation or I&A | Confirmed | Medium | SAFe Compliance | Plan IP iteration for PI 7; schedule I&A ceremony at PI 6 close |

## 12. SAFe Compliance Assessment

### 12.1 SAFe Core Values

| Core Value | Status | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| **Alignment** | Partial | PI Objectives exist and are typed correctly. However, 50% are "New" in the final iteration, suggesting weak traceability from objectives to sprint work. |
| **Built-in Quality** | Unknown | No evidence of Definition of Done, acceptance criteria, or peer review tasks in the sprint backlog. |
| **Transparency** | Poor | Capacity = 0 makes burndown invisible. Risks/Impediments backlog is empty. Stale items obscure true backlog health. |
| **Program Execution** | Partial | Iteration cadence is maintained (2-week sprints). But work isn't flowing — 59% still "New" on Day 2. |

### 12.2 SAFe Essential Practices

| Practice | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Iteration Planning | Partial | Items assigned to sprint, but capacity not set and many items are "New" — suggests items were added without a formal planning ceremony |
| Definition of Ready | Not Enforced | 1 item in "Grooming" state is in the sprint |
| Capacity-Based Planning | Not Implemented | 0 hrs/day configured |
| WIP Limits | Not Visible | No evidence of WIP limits on the board |
| Daily Stand-up | Unknown | Ceremonies are not tracked in ADO |
| Iteration Review/Demo | Unknown | Sprint ends Mar 8 — no evidence of scheduling |
| Iteration Retrospective | Unknown | No evidence in ADO |
| Story Point Estimation | Mostly Implemented | 88% estimated (15 of 17 items) |
| WSJF Prioritization | Not Implemented | Effort and Business Value fields exist but are empty on all 36 Features |
| PI Objectives | Implemented | 6 PI Objectives defined — good practice |
| ROAM Risk Management | Not Implemented | 0 risks/impediments logged |
| Backlog Refinement | Partial | Items in "Grooming" state suggest some refinement occurs, but stale items from 8 months ago indicate it is not systematic |
| Inspect & Adapt | Unknown | No IP iteration in PI 6; no I&A artifacts visible |

---

## 13. Recommendations

### 13.1 Immediate Actions (This Week — Days 2–5 of Sprint)

| # | Action | Owner | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Configure team capacity** for armelita, Samantha Babael, and Teofilo Limpag with actual hours/day | Scrum Master / Team Lead | Critical |
| 2 | **Add Samantha Babael and Teofilo Limpag** to the JIT Operation Team roster in Azure DevOps | Project Admin | Critical |
| 3 | **Clarify TESDA AC compliance deadline** — if there is a hard regulatory date, the 7 compliance items (IDs: 199496–199503) must be prioritized above all other work | armelita | High |
| 4 | **Resolve item 198637** (Markdown Training Dry-run) — complete grooming and move to "Ready" or defer to next sprint | Samantha Babael | Medium |
| 5 | **Estimate 2 unestimated items** (198630 Markdown Training, 199221 ChatGPT Courseware) | Team | Medium |
| 6 | **Move "New" items to "Active"** as work begins — update board states daily to reflect actual progress | All team members | Medium |

### 13.2 Before Sprint End (March 8, 2026)

| # | Action | Owner | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | **Record sprint actuals** — document how many of the 23 story points were completed; this becomes the team's first velocity baseline | Scrum Master | High |
| 8 | **Update PI Objective states** — reflect true progress before PI 6 closes | armelita | High |
| 9 | **Conduct Iteration Review** — demo completed work to stakeholders, especially TESDA compliance deliverables | Team | Medium |
| 10 | **Conduct Iteration Retrospective** — discuss: Why is capacity not configured? Is the workload distribution sustainable? Are items entering sprints in "Ready" state? | Team | Medium |

### 13.3 For PI 7 Planning

| # | Action | Owner | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| 11 | **Redistribute workload** — target no single team member owning more than 40% of sprint items | Scrum Master | High |
| 12 | **Conduct backlog grooming** — review and remove/re-plan 20 stale items from PI 3 and root iteration | Team | High |
| 13 | **Include an IP iteration** in PI 7 — protect time for innovation, hackathons, and Inspect & Adapt | RTE / Program Manager | High |
| 14 | **Populate Effort and Business Value** on all Features to enable WSJF prioritization | Product Owner | Medium |
| 15 | **Start logging risks** in the Risks and Impediments backlog — use ROAM categorization | Team | Medium |
| 16 | **Establish velocity baseline** — use Iteration 6.4 actuals + first 2 sprints of PI 7 to calculate average velocity for future planning | Scrum Master | Medium |

### 13.4 Recommendation Priority Matrix

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Recommendation Priority — Effort vs Impact
    x-axis Low Effort --> High Effort
    y-axis Low Impact --> High Impact
    quadrant-1 Plan & Schedule
    quadrant-2 Do First
    quadrant-3 Defer
    quadrant-4 Quick Wins
    "Configure Capacity": [0.15, 0.90]
    "Add Team Members to Roster": [0.10, 0.70]
    "Clarify TESDA Deadline": [0.20, 0.85]
    "Update Board States Daily": [0.10, 0.60]
    "Record Velocity Baseline": [0.25, 0.75]
    "Redistribute Workload": [0.65, 0.90]
    "Backlog Grooming (Stale)": [0.55, 0.65]
    "Populate WSJF Fields": [0.70, 0.60]
    "Add IP Iteration to PI 7": [0.40, 0.70]
    "Start ROAM Risk Tracking": [0.30, 0.55]
```

---

## 14. Conclusion

The JIT Operation Team demonstrates **promising SAFe fundamentals** — properly typed PI Objectives, story-driven delivery (82% User Stories), reasonable story point adoption (88%), and right-sized work items (1–3 points). These are signs of a team that understands the mechanics of agile delivery.

However, **critical process gaps** prevent the team from realizing the benefits of these fundamentals:

1. **Zero configured capacity** makes planning and tracking meaningless
2. **76% workload concentration on one person** creates unacceptable organizational risk
3. **No risk management** leaves the team blind to impediments
4. **Stale backlog items** (8+ months) indicate insufficient refinement discipline
5. **No IP iteration** removes protected time for improvement

The path forward is clear: **fix capacity and workload balance first** (Quick Wins), then **establish velocity and refinement cadence** (Short Term), and finally **implement WSJF and formalize I&A** (Medium Term). The team has the foundation — it now needs the discipline.

---

*Audit report generated by Claude AI Agile PM Consultant on 2026-02-24. Data sourced live from Azure DevOps (`dev.azure.com/jairo`, Jairosoft Portfolio project, JIT Operation Team board, Iteration 6.4). Based on SAFe 6.0 framework standards from [ScaledAgileFramework.com](https://ScaledAgileFramework.com).*

file.content:
# SAFe Iteration Audit Report — JIT Operation Team, Iteration 6.4

**Audit Date:** 2026-02-24
**Auditor:** Claude AI (Agile PM Consultant, 10+ years SAFe experience)
**Framework:** Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) 6.0
**Organization:** Jairosoft LLC (`dev.azure.com/jairo`)
**Project:** Jairosoft Portfolio
**Team:** JIT Operation Team
**Scope:** Iteration 6.4 (February 23 – March 8, 2026)
**PI Context:** 2026-PI6, Final Iteration (4 of 4)

---

## 1. Executive Summary

This audit examines the **JIT Operation Team's** board and current sprint (Iteration 6.4) within the Jairosoft Portfolio project. The JIT Operation Team is classified as a **Shared Services** team responsible for training operations, TESDA certification/compliance, courseware development, intern programs, and partnerships for the Jairo Institute of Technology.

The team is in **Day 2 of a 14-day sprint** with 17 work items committed totaling 23 story points. While the sprint demonstrates strong fundamentals — story-driven delivery, proper estimation, and right-sized work items — critical process gaps in capacity planning, workload balance, and risk management severely undermine execution predictability.

### Overall Iteration Health

| Dimension | Score | Rating |
|---|---|---|
| Capacity Planning | 1/10 | Critical |
| Workload Balance | 2/10 | Critical |
| Risk Management | 1/10 | Critical |
| State Discipline | 4/10 | Poor |
| Sprint Scope | 5/10 | Moderate |
| Estimation Quality | 7/10 | Good |
| Work Item Types | 8/10 | Strong |
| Story Point Adoption | 8/10 | Strong |

**Overall Iteration Score: 4.5 / 10 — Needs Significant Improvement**

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Iteration 6.4 Sprint Health — JIT Operation Team
    x-axis Low Health --> High Health
    y-axis Low Risk --> High Risk
    quadrant-1 Fix Immediately
    quadrant-2 Monitor Closely
    quadrant-3 Acceptable
    quadrant-4 Improve Next Sprint
    "Capacity Planning": [0.05, 0.90]
    "Workload Balance": [0.15, 0.85]
    "Story Point Adoption": [0.80, 0.20]
    "Work Item Types": [0.75, 0.25]
    "State Discipline": [0.30, 0.70]
    "Risk Management": [0.10, 0.65]
    "Sprint Scope": [0.45, 0.55]
    "Estimation Quality": [0.70, 0.30]
```

---

## 2. Team Profile

### 2.1 Team Roster (Azure DevOps)

The JIT Operation Team has **2 registered members** in Azure DevOps:

| Member | Email | Registered | Capacity (Iteration 6.4) |
|---|---|---|---|
| armelita | armelita@jairosoft.com | Yes | **0 hrs/day** |
| grace | grace@jairosoft.com | Yes | **0 hrs/day** |

**Additional contributors** (not registered as team members):

| Contributor | Items in Sprint | Registered |
|---|---|---|
| Samantha Babael | 3 | No |
| Teofilo Limpag | 1 | No |

**Total team capacity configured: 0 hrs/day**

### 2.2 Board Configuration

The team's board is configured with **6 backlog levels**:

| Backlog Level | Work Item Types | Visible | Items |
|---|---|---|---|
| Goals and Releases | Goal, Release | Hidden | -- |
| Epics and Objectives | Epic, PI Objective | Visible | 6 |
| Features and Enablers | Feature, Enabler Feature, Change Request | Visible | 36 |
| Stories and Deliverables | User Story, Enabler, Spike, Courseware, Training, Design, Defect, Tech Debt | Visible | 42 |
| Risks and Impediments | Issue, Risk | Visible | **0** |
| Tasks | Task, Peer Review, Bug | Visible | -- |

**Total board work items: 84**

---

## 3. PI 6 Context — PI Objectives

The JIT Operation Team has **6 PI Objectives** defined for 2026-PI6. All are typed as "PI Objective" at the Epic level — a correct SAFe practice.

| ID | PI Objective | State | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|
| 197441 | Produce Professional Alumni | Active | armelita |
| 197443 | Recognized as CSS NC II Assessment Center | Active | armelita |
| 197445 | Launch new courses | Active | armelita |
| 197444 | CSS NC II Assessment Offering | New | Unassigned |
| 197446 | Use OpenEduCat for JIT Operations | New | armelita |
| 197450 | Onboard Qualified Interns for eLMS and jit.edu.ph | New | armelita |

```mermaid
pie title PI 6 Objective States
    "Active" : 3
    "New" : 3
```

**Finding:** In the **final iteration of PI 6**, 3 of 6 PI Objectives (50%) remain in "New" state. Per SAFe, PI Objectives should be actively worked toward throughout the PI. Objectives still in "New" in the last iteration suggest either:
- The objectives were not properly decomposed into Features/Stories
- Work started but the objective status was never updated
- The objectives are at risk of not being met this PI

**Recommendation:** Before PI 6 closes, update all PI Objective states to reflect reality. During the upcoming Inspect & Adapt, formally assess which objectives were met vs. missed.

---

## 4. Iteration Structure — PI 6 Timeline

The JIT Operation Team is subscribed to iterations spanning PI 3 through PI 6:

### PI 6 Iteration Schedule

```mermaid
gantt
    title 2026-PI6 — JIT Operation Team Iterations
    dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD
    section PI 6
    Iteration 6.1 (Completed)   :done, 2026-01-12, 2026-01-25
    Iteration 6.2 (Completed)   :done, 2026-01-26, 2026-02-08
    Iteration 6.3 (Completed)   :done, 2026-02-09, 2026-02-22
    Iteration 6.4 (CURRENT)     :active, 2026-02-23, 2026-03-08
```

| Iteration | Start | End | Duration | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 6.1 | Jan 12, 2026 | Jan 25, 2026 | 2 weeks | Completed |
| 6.2 | Jan 26, 2026 | Feb 08, 2026 | 2 weeks | Completed |
| 6.3 | Feb 09, 2026 | Feb 22, 2026 | 2 weeks | Completed |
| **6.4** | **Feb 23, 2026** | **Mar 08, 2026** | **2 weeks** | **Current** |

**Finding:** PI 6 has **4 iterations** (8 weeks) instead of the standard SAFe 5+1 IP pattern (12 weeks). Earlier PIs (PI 3, PI 4) had 5–6 iterations each. This shortened PI may indicate:
- A deliberate cadence change
- Incomplete iteration configuration
- Missing Innovation & Planning (IP) iteration

**SAFe Note:** The absence of a dedicated IP iteration means no protected time for innovation, planning, and Inspect & Adapt. This should be addressed for PI 7.

### 6.3 Finding — Critical Workload Imbalance

**armelita carries 76% of all sprint work (13 items, 18 of 23 story points).** This is the single most critical finding in this audit.

| Risk Factor | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Bus Factor** | If armelita is unavailable for even 2–3 days, 76% of the sprint is at risk |

| **Burnout Risk** | 18 story points in 10 working days = ~1.8 pts/day sustained |
| **Quality Risk** | High volume + single person = reduced time for thorough work |

**SAFe Guidance:** SAFe recommends work be distributed across team members to promote collective ownership and reduce dependency risk. No single team member should own more than 30–40% of a sprint's committed work.

## 14. Conclusion

The JIT Operation Team demonstrates **promising SAFe fundamentals** — properly typed PI Objectives, story-driven delivery (82% User Stories), reasonable story point adoption (88%), and right-sized work items (1–3 points). These are signs of a team that understands the mechanics of agile delivery.

However, **critical process gaps** prevent the team from realizing the benefits of these fundamentals:

1. **Zero configured capacity** makes planning and tracking meaningless
2. **76% workload concentration on one person** creates unacceptable organizational risk
3. **No risk management** leaves the team blind to impediments
4. **Stale backlog items** (8+ months) indicate insufficient refinement discipline
5. **No IP iteration** removes protected time for improvement

The path forward is clear: **fix capacity and workload balance first** (Quick Wins), then **establish velocity and refinement cadence** (Short Term), and finally **implement WSJF and formalize I&A** (Medium Term). The team has the foundation — it now needs the discipline.

---

Based on SAFe 6.0 framework standards from [ScaledAgileFramework.com](https://ScaledAgileFramework.com).*

# SAFe Iteration Audit Report — JIT Operation Team, Iteration 6.4

**Audit Date:** 2026-02-24
**Auditor:** Claude AI (Agile PM Consultant, 10+ years SAFe experience)
**Framework:** Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) 6.0
**Organization:** Jairosoft LLC (`dev.azure.com/jairo`)
**Project:** Jairosoft Portfolio
**Team:** JIT Operation Team
**Scope:** Iteration 6.4 (February 23 – March 8, 2026)
**PI Context:** 2026-PI6, Final Iteration (4 of 4)

---

## 1. Executive Summary

This audit examines the **JIT Operation Team's** board and current sprint (Iteration 6.4) within the Jairosoft Portfolio project. The JIT Operation Team is classified as a **Shared Services** team responsible for training operations, TESDA certification/compliance, courseware development, intern programs, and partnerships for the Jairo Institute of Technology.

The team is in **Day 2 of a 14-day sprint** with 17 work items committed totaling 23 story points. While the sprint demonstrates strong fundamentals — story-driven delivery, proper estimation, and right-sized work items — critical process gaps in capacity planning, workload balance, and risk management severely undermine execution predictability.

### Overall Iteration Health

| Dimension | Score | Rating |
|---|---|---|
| Capacity Planning | 1/10 | Critical |
| Workload Balance | 2/10 | Critical |
| Risk Management | 1/10 | Critical |
| State Discipline | 4/10 | Poor |
| Sprint Scope | 5/10 | Moderate |
| Estimation Quality | 7/10 | Good |
| Work Item Types | 8/10 | Strong |
| Story Point Adoption | 8/10 | Strong |

**Overall Iteration Score: 4.5 / 10 — Needs Significant Improvement**

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Iteration 6.4 Sprint Health — JIT Operation Team
    x-axis Low Health --> High Health
    y-axis Low Risk --> High Risk
    quadrant-1 Fix Immediately
    quadrant-2 Monitor Closely
    quadrant-3 Acceptable
    quadrant-4 Improve Next Sprint
    "Capacity Planning": [0.05, 0.90]
    "Workload Balance": [0.15, 0.85]
    "Story Point Adoption": [0.80, 0.20]
    "Work Item Types": [0.75, 0.25]
    "State Discipline": [0.30, 0.70]
    "Risk Management": [0.10, 0.65]
    "Sprint Scope": [0.45, 0.55]
    "Estimation Quality": [0.70, 0.30]
```

|---|---|

| **Quality Risk** | High volume + single person = reduced time for thorough work |

**SAFe Guidance:** SAFe recommends work be distributed across team members to promote collective ownership and reduce dependency risk. No single team member should own more than 30–40% of a sprint's committed work.

## 14. Conclusion

The JIT Operation Team demonstrates **promising SAFe fundamentals** — properly typed PI Objectives, story-driven delivery (82% User Stories), reasonable story point adoption (88%), and right-sized work items (1–3 points). These are signs of a team that understands the mechanics of agile delivery.

However, **critical process gaps** prevent the team from realizing the benefits of these fundamentals:

1. **Zero configured capacity** makes planning and tracking meaningless
2. **76% workload concentration on one person** creates unacceptable organizational risk
3. **No risk management** leaves the team blind to impediments
4. **Stale backlog items** (8+ months) indicate insufficient refinement discipline
5. **No IP iteration** removes protected time for improvement

The path forward is clear: **fix capacity and workload balance first** (Quick Wins), then **establish velocity and refinement cadence** (Short Term), and finally **implement WSJF and formalize I&A** (Medium Term). The team has the foundation — it now needs the discipline.
